messages.content:
The following are the expected laboratory results of
iron deficiency anemia:
A. Low VMCV, L RBC, Low TIBC
B. Low MCV. Low RBC. Increased TIBC
C. Low MCV. normal RBC, Increased TIBC
D. Low MCV. Low RBC. normal TIBC
B. Low MCV. Low RBC. Increased TIBC.

Which of the following condition does NOT require a
low protein diet?
A. PKU
B. Citrullinemia
C. MSUD
D. Galactosemia
D. Galactosemia does not require a low protein diet.

One of the ff is the most frequent cause of ALTB:
A. RSV
B. Adenovirus
C. Para influenza virus
D. Influenza A
C. Para influenza virus is the most frequent cause of acute lower respiratory tract infections (ALRTI).

Regurgitation is common manifestation among patient
with:
A. Malabsorption
B. Lactose Intolerance
C. GERD
D. Nasal obstruction
C. GERD (gastroesophageal reflux disease) is commonly associated with regurgitation.

One of the ff is the most frequent cause of ALTB:
A. RSV
B. Adenovirus
C. Para influenza virus
D. Influenza A
C. Para influenza virus is the most frequent cause of acute lower respiratory tract infections (ALRTI).

Regurgitation is common manifestation among patient
with:
A. Malabsorption
B. Lactose Intolerance
C. GERD
D. Nasal obstruction
C. GERD (gastroesophageal reflux disease) is commonly associated with regurgitation.

When doing the advance ventilation for a patient in
impending respiratory failure, which of the following
maneuver can lower aspiration risk us minimize or
prevent aspiration of gastric contents
A. Sellick's maneuver
B. Head-tilt chin-lift
C. C-E technique
D. Jaw thrust
A. Sellick's maneuver can lower the aspiration risk and minimize or prevent aspiration of gastric contents during advanced ventilation.

One of the following is a hallmark of Croup among
infants and children
A. Barking cough
B. Wet cough
C. Whooping cough
D. Dry cough
A. Barking cough is a hallmark of croup among infants and children.

A 1 yo male child has a history of 3 days watery
diarrhea. He appears pale and has an RR of 65
breaths per minute, HR of 200 beats per minute, and
a systolic blood pressure of 50 mmHg. His extremities
are cool and mottled with a capillary refill time of >2
seconds. After providing 100% oxygen, what is the
best next thing to do?
A. Insert advance ET as eventually this will lead
to respiratory failure
B. Start IV line and give epinephrine at
0.1mg/kg thru IV
C. Insert IV line and give bolus of isotonic
solution 20cc/kg
D. Attach to the monitor to check for the cardiac
rhythm

C. Insert IV line and give bolus of isotonic solution 20cc/kg. The child is showing signs of dehydration and hypovolemic shock, so the priority action is to rehydrate the child by giving a bolus of isotonic solution. Inserting an ET will not be necessary at this point, epinephrine is not indicated because there is no indication of cardiac arrest at this time, and the cardiac rhythm can be assessed after the child has been stabilized.
